content.full_text:
COUNTY COUNSEL
108 COURT STREET • JACKSON, CA 95642-2379 • (209) 223-6366 • FAX (209) 223-4286

June 13, 1991

Ms. Deanne Stone
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, #800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Request for Formal Advice [2 CA Code of Regs. 18329(b)]

Dear Ms. Stone:

On March 20, 1991, I requested advice on behalf of Amador County
Supervisor Tim Davenport as to whether or not he might have a
conflict of interest relating to a request for a rate increase from
a garbage hauling company, NorCal. An employee of NorCal owns
improved real property with Supervisor Davenport as tenants in
common. The facts are set forth in detail in my March 20 letter.

You responded with a letter dated April 15, 1991, in which you
cited 2 CA Code of Regs. 18329(b)(8)(A) which provides that formal
written advice must be declined where the requestor is seeking
advice relating to past conduct. You went on to suggest that if
Supervisor Davenport should require the FPPC's guidance regarding
future board decisions involving Mr. Molinelli or his employee
NorCal, the FPPC would be pleased to provide that advice. I am now
requesting that advice.

The board of supervisors will be considering in the near future the
following issues:

1. On July 2, 1991, the board will hear and decide upon
adopting a resolution of public use and necessity to acquire by
eminent domain the currently leased Amador County landfill. The
acquisition of the landfill by eminent domain will involve the
expenditure of County funds as authorized by the board. These
costs are as yet unknown but will be substantial. Those costs will
be passed on to garbage haulers in the County, including NorCal,
who will no doubt request a rate hike to offset those costs.

2. Matters concerning other garbage haulers arise
continually. Each franchise hauler has an exclusive territory.
Issues involving a franchise not owned by NorCal might set a
precedent for the board's decision on a similar issue relating to
Norcal even though there is no legal linkage between the two. An
example is other franchise haulers' rates.

3. Similar to other jurisdictions, Amador County is awash
with proposals for recycling waste. Some of those proposals will
affect all garbage haulers because the proposals will establish de
facto garbage haulers in the form of civic organizations or

commercial enterprises doing recycling which will have a financial
impact on NorCal. In addition, proposals soon to be considered by
the board will require NorCal and other garbage haulers to recycle
solid waste. Any recycling program will have a material effect on
the garbage haulers' revenues and/or costs.

4. Currently the board has before it a challenge to one of
NorCal's franchises on the basis that the franchise was transferred
from a predecessor in interest to NorCal without the board's
approval, which approval is required by the franchise itself. That
challenge comes from a garbage hauler who has no County franchise
and is demanding that the board commence a bidding process for
NorCal's franchise. Obviously, the financial impact to NorCal is
enormous. That matter is currently being reviewed by the board's
landfill committee and by me; it will shortly go to the full board.
In addition, the challenger is requesting that the board disallow
NorCal's exclusive right to collect and haul garbage in one
franchise area. This is very, very current. That has a
significant impact on NorCal's revenue.

5. Finally, assuming that Supervisor Davenport has a
conflict of interest on any given issue, is he precluded (a) as a
supervisor and/or (b) as a private citizen from discussing with or
addressing other board members on the issue?

I cannot impress upon you enough how frequently issues involving
solid waste, the landfill, garbage haulers generally and NorCal
specifically arise formally or informally before the board. Amador
County is very small and rural. The services provided by garbage
haulers such as NorCal are a "big ticket" cost items to the county,
rate payers, and the franchises themselves. Supervisor Davenport
has been reluctant to take part in any discussion involving any
matter involving solid waste, the landfill, garbage haulers other
than NorCal, and NorCal itself because of the possibility that his
financial relationship with Mr. Molinelli will rise to the level of
a legally cognizable conflict of interest. Thus, on many, many
issues before the board Supervisor Davenport does not take part,
thus reducing his effectiveness as a representative and the board's
as well.

Thank you in advance for your help.

Yours,very truly,

JOHN F. HAHN
County Counsel

JFH/p

sections.question:
1. On July 2, 1991, the board will hear and decide upon adopting a resolution of public use and necessity to acquire by eminent domain the currently leased Amador County landfill. The acquisition of the landfill by eminent domain will involve the expenditure of County funds as authorized by the board. Those costs are as yet unknown but will be substantial. Those costs will be passed on to garbage haulers in the County, including NorCal, who will no doubt request a rate hike to offset those costs.

2. Matters concerning other garbage haulers arise continually. Each franchise hauler has an exclusive territory. Issues involving a franchise not owned by NorCal might set a precedent for the board's decision on a similar issue relating to NorCal even though there is no legal linkage between the two. An example is other franchise haulers' rates.

3. Similar to other jurisdictions, Amador County is awash with proposals for recycling waste. Some of those proposals will affect all garbage haulers because the proposals will establish de facto garbage haulers in the form of civic organizations or commercial enterprises doing recycling which will have a financial impact on NorCal. In addition, proposals soon to be considered by the board will require NorCal and other garbage haulers to recycle solid waste. Any recycling program will have a material effect on the garbage haulers, revenues and/or costs.

4. Currently the board has before it a challenge to one of NorCal's franchises on the basis that the franchise has transferred from a predecessor in interest to NorCal without the board's approval, which approval is required by the franchise itself. That challenge comes from a garbage hauler who has no County franchise and is demanding that the board commence a bidding process for NorCal's franchise. Obviously, the financial impact to NorCal is enormous.

5. Finally, assuming that Supervisor Davenport has a conflict of interest on any given issue, is he precluded (a) as a supervisor and/or (b) as a private citizen from discussing with or addressing other board members on the issue?
sections.question_synthetic: Whether Amador County Supervisor Tim Davenport, who has a tenancy-in-common financial interest with an employee of garbage hauling company NorCal, has a conflict of interest regarding multiple board decisions affecting NorCal and solid waste management. Additionally, whether a supervisor with a conflict of interest is precluded from discussing or addressing other board members on such issues.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter to FPPC, not an FPPC response letter. The letter references a prior FPPC response dated April 15, 1991 that declined to provide formal written advice on past conduct, and this letter represents a follow-up request for advice regarding future conduct. No CONCLUSION section is present in this document.

embedding.summary: County Counsel for Amador County requests formal FPPC advice regarding whether Supervisor Tim Davenport has a conflict of interest relating to multiple upcoming board decisions involving NorCal, a garbage hauling company, given his financial interest as a tenant in common with an NorCal employee. The request details five specific categories of decisions that may arise and asks whether a supervisor with a conflict of interest is precluded from participating in discussions.
